macro_segments.title: Introduction to Daf 57 and Overview of Perek's Transition from Specific Objects to Conceptual Framework

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks on switching from specific object inclusions to conceptual mishna about konam vows

macro_segments.title: The Mishna on Konam: Foundation of Neder Based on Korban Model and Extension to Chilufim and Gidulim

micro_segments.title: Konam formula and basic restriction; connection to korban status and extension to exchanges and growth
Theoretical basis: how konam draws from hekdesh paradigm; chilufim and gidulim in korbanot context
Comparison of neder to issurei hana'ah; specific areas where exchanges become forbidden; me'ilah by konamot

macro_segments.title: Distinction Between Konam and Action-Based Language: Impact on Chilufim and Gidulim Forbidden Status

micro_segments.title: When action-based language (she'ani ochel, she'ani to'em) is used, exchanges and growth become permissible
State vs. action distinction: why generic 'forbidden' differs from specific actions like eating or tasting

macro_segments.title: Zaro Kalah and Ein Zaro Kalah: Two Categories of Growth and Their Different Forbidden Status

micro_segments.title: Zaro kalah scenario: seed/core goes out of existence; gidulim permissible even with ochel/to'em language
Ein zaro kalah scenario: original persists and grows (e.g., fruit on tree, onion); gidulim forbidden even with ochel/to'em
Gidulei gidulim question and clarification of why it applies only to zaro kalah

macro_segments.title: Wife's Labor Vow with Temporal Qualifier: Position of Modifier Determines Scope of Prohibition

micro_segments.title: First case: 'until Pesach I won't benefit' — temporal modifier restricts duration, not scope of objects produced
Second case: 'what you produce until Pesach I won't benefit' — temporal modifier restricts which objects are included
Scribal omission noted; full mishna text reread including preliminary clause about husband forbidding wife's work
Wife's labor vow with konam; chilufim and gidulim forbidden; Ran's ruling on raw material costs

macro_segments.title: Conditional Vow: Benefit Forbidden Until Pesach If Wife Visits Father Until Succos

micro_segments.title: Scenario introduced: two time-frames with condition (visiting father until Succos) and prohibition (no benefit until Pesach)
Easy case: wife visits before Pesach, triggers condition within prohibition period, clearly violates vow
Complex case: wife benefits before Pesach but triggers condition after, retroactively transgresses lo yachel devaro
Discussion of whether wife may benefit lechatchila before Pesach assuming no condition trigger; rabbinic concern about condition seriousness
Ran's distinction between Rambam and his own position on who transgresses lo yachel devaro in non-self vows

macro_segments.title: Conditional Vow Second Variant: Condition Until Pesach, Prohibition Until Succos

micro_segments.title: Second conditional case: 'until Succos I forbid benefit if you visit father until Pesach'; condition expires first
If visit occurs before Pesach, remains forbidden through Succos; if visit after Pesach, permissible

macro_segments.title: Gemara on Gidulim: Does Growth Nullify via Bittul or Remain Forbidden? Onion and Orlah Cases

micro_segments.title: Yishmael of Kfar Yama raises question: onion uprooted in shvi'it year, replanted in eighth year, grew majority; does growth nullify via bittul?
Ran interprets 'he brought up' as theoretical question, not literal act; discusses rov as non-precise term
Question formulated: if gidulim are היתר, can they negate (be mevatel) the original issur? Or is gidulim itself issur?
Rabbi Ami has no answer; Rabbi Yitzchak Naphcha cites Rabbi Chanina Treita on terumah onion: heter; seems permissible
Rabbi Yirmiyah challenges citing only one opinion; presents two rabbis saying issur: Rabbi Yochanan on orlah grafting case
Rabbi Yochanan's orlah case: grafted young branch with fruit onto old tree; fruit grew 200-fold; remains assur despite bittul amount
Rabbi Yonatan's kilayim case: onion planted in vineyard, then vineyard uprooted; original assur, growth permissible; remains assur

macro_segments.title: Gemara Discussion of Bittul via Ma'asrot Obligation: Can New Growth Negate Original Status to Allow Lenient Ruling?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yitzchak in name of Rabbi Yochanan: liter of onions with ma'asrot separated, then planted and grown; do we take ma'asrot from all or only new?
Halacha: ma'asrot taken from total amount suggests new growth nullifies original for obligation purposes
Objection: stringency to impose ma'asrot obligation doesn't prove leniency to nullify original issur via bittul
Inconclusion: tension between stringent and lenient applications of bittul remains unresolved; to be continued

topical_tags.term: zaro kalah (seed goes out of existence)
lo yachel devaro (do not void your words)
